edges.description: Loss of normal NBN function delays and weakens p53 damage signaling.
DNA damage response failure perturbs neurodevelopmental homeostasis.
NBN-dependent DNA repair failure also produces systemic chromosomal instability, immunodeficiency, and lymphoid malignancy susceptibility.
Delayed p53 signaling contributes to abnormal neural development.
Abnormal neurogenesis progresses to neuronal loss in NBS organoids.
Neuronal loss contributes to progressive microcephaly.
Systemic chromosomal instability and NBS disease biology are associated with growth retardation.
DNA repair failure and chromosomal instability in NBS are associated with combined cellular and humoral immunodeficiency.
Genomic instability and immune dysregulation create strong susceptibility to lymphoid malignancy.

nodes.description: Defective DNA damage signaling contributes to disrupted cerebral organoid architecture and premature neural differentiation.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: The organoids exhibit premature differentiation, and Neuronatin (NNAT) over-expression.

nodes.description: NBN-related DNA repair failure produces chromosomal instability with systemic growth, immune, and lymphoid-malignancy manifestations beyond the neurodevelopmental branch.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Hallmarks of NBS include chromosomal instability and clinical manifestations such as growth retardation, immunodeficiency, and progressive microcephaly.

Combined cellular and humoral immunodeficiency with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, a strong predisposition to develop malignancies (predominantly of lymphoid origin) and radiosensitivity are other integral manifestations of the syndrome.

nodes.description: Growth retardation is a major clinical manifestation of Nijmegen breakage syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Hallmarks of NBS include chromosomal instability and clinical manifestations such as growth retardation, immunodeficiency, and progressive microcephaly.

The principal clinical manifestations of the syndrome are: microcephaly, present at birth and progressive with age, dysmorphic facial features, mild growth retardation, mild-to-moderate intellectual disability, and, in females, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.

nodes.description: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation may be considered in selected patients with severe immunodeficiency or related complications.

evidence.snippet: No specific therapy is available for NBS, however, hematopoietic stem cell transplantation may be one option for some patients.

nodes.description: Immunodeficiency is a core non-neurologic hallmark of Nijmegen breakage syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Hallmarks of NBS include chromosomal instability and clinical manifestations such as growth retardation, immunodeficiency, and progressive microcephaly.

Combined cellular and humoral immunodeficiency with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, a strong predisposition to develop malignancies (predominantly of lymphoid origin) and radiosensitivity are other integral manifestations of the syndrome.

nodes.description: Patient-derived organoids show delayed p53 signaling after genotoxic stress, linking NBN dysfunction to defective checkpoint activation.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: After exposure to bleomycin, NBS organoids undergo delayed p53-mediated DNA damage response

nodes.description: Nijmegen breakage syndrome confers marked susceptibility to lymphoid malignancy, which is a major determinant of prognosis.

evidence.snippet: Combined cellular and humoral immunodeficiency with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, a strong predisposition to develop malignancies (predominantly of lymphoid origin) and radiosensitivity are other integral manifestations of the syndrome.

nodes.description: Progressive microcephaly is one of the classic hallmarks of Nijmegen breakage syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Hallmarks of NBS include chromosomal instability and clinical manifestations such as growth retardation, immunodeficiency, and progressive microcephaly.

The principal clinical manifestations of the syndrome are: microcephaly, present at birth and progressive with age, dysmorphic facial features, mild growth retardation, mild-to-moderate intellectual disability, and, in females, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome (NBS) is a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by mutations within nibrin (NBN), a DNA damage repair protein.
NBN | HGNC:7652 | Nijmegen breakage syndrome | MONDO:0009623 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Pathogenic NBN variants impair DNA damage repair, establishing the core molecular lesion in Nijmegen breakage syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome (NBS) is a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by mutations within nibrin (NBN), a DNA damage repair protein.

nodes.description: Delayed DNA-damage signaling and aberrant neuronal differentiation culminate in neuronal loss.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired p53-Mediated DNA Damage Response Contributes to Microcephaly in Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome Patient-Derived Cerebral Organoids
evidence.snippet: After exposure to bleomycin, NBS organoids undergo delayed p53-mediated DNA damage response and aberrant trans-synaptic signaling, which ultimately leads to neuronal apoptosis.
